key_insight: Swift heavy ion impact in Al2O3 creates a radially dependent band-gap collapse, with metallization near the ion track that strongly alters how energy is transferred to the lattice.
engineering_relevance: Accounting for spatially varying electronic structure near the track improves predictive damage models for ion irradiation of insulating ceramics and helps estimate track formation thresholds.
method: TREKIS Monte Carlo simulates initial electronic and ionic excitation after the ion passage
DFT-based molecular dynamics tracks band-structure evolution in the ion track
The two methods are coupled to map radius-dependent band-gap collapse to local atomic heating
evidence_signal: For a 700 MeV Bi ion, the abstract reports metallization within about 2 nm of the trajectory, a reduced gap at 3–5 nm, and little effect beyond 5 nm, which leads to more discontinuous damage patterns than a uniform-energy-delivery model.
watch_item: The result is model-dependent and may be sensitive to the chosen coupling between electronic excitation, band-gap evolution, and lattice heating, so transferability to other ions or materials needs validation.
paper_title: Effect of radially heterogeneous band gap collapse on formation of swift heavy ion tracks in Al2O3
